title: NIS2 Art.23(4)(a) — 24-Hour Early-Warning Notification Readiness
industry: All sectors in NIS2 Annex I and II

compliance.assurance_rationale: This UC addresses Art.23(4)(a) specifically — the 24-hour early-warning obligation — and thereby contributes to the overall Art.23 incident-notification framework.
compliance.controlObjective: Contributes evidence toward the Art.23 incident-reporting lifecycle by monitoring the 24-hour early-warning clock for significant incidents.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search UC-22.2.1 row-per-finding output archived to index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:saved_search.

compliance.assurance_rationale: The UC produces auditor-ready Splunk evidence that the SOC has visibility on the 24-hour early-warning clock for every significant incident. Legal classification and the actual filing to the CSIRT/competent authority remain outside Splunk and require human approval, hence partial.
compliance.controlObjective: Proves that for every significant incident raised in ES, the 24-hour early-warning clock is monitored, an owner is assigned, and the early-warning package is drafted within deadline.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search `UC-22.2.1` writing one row per high/critical notable approaching or breaching the 24-hour clock to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:saved_search`, signed via RFC 3161 TSA. Daily digest archived to `index=audit_evidence` with 7-year retention. Dashboard panel `NIS2 Art.23 — 24h clock` on the NIS2 posture dashboard.

dataSources: ES `notable` macro (resolves to `index=notable eventtype=notable` in ES <7.0 or the KV-store-backed `incident_review` collection in ES 7.0+). Auto-extracted fields: `rule_name`, `urgency` (informational/low/medium/high/critical), `status` (Unassigned/New/In Progress/Pending/Resolved/Closed), `owner`, `status_description`, `comment`, `risk_object`, `risk_object_type`, `src`, `dest`, `category`, `severity`, `risk_score`, `_time` (notable creation time). Optional join: `index=phantom sourcetype=phantom:container container_id=*` for SOAR cases linked via `nis2_incident_link.csv`. Reference lookup: `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv` (clause-tier threshold mapping per the operator's NIS2 self-classification).
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) ≥7.0; Splunk Add-on for Phantom (Splunkbase 3411) ≥3.0 for SOAR enrichment; Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621) ≥5.3 for the Alerts data model.
spl:
`notable` urgency IN ("high","critical") earliest=-3d
| eval hours_since_detection = round((now() - _time) / 3600, 2)
| lookup nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv rule_name OUTPUT nis2_tier nis2_significant
| eval nis2_significant = coalesce(nis2_significant, "unclassified")
| where nis2_significant != "no" AND status != "Closed"
| eval clock_state = case(
    hours_since_detection >= 24, "BREACH",
    hours_since_detection >= 20, "WARN_T_MINUS_4H",
    hours_since_detection >= 12, "NOTICE_T_MINUS_12H",
    1==1, "OK")
| where clock_state != "OK"
| lookup nis2_exception_register.csv rule_name OUTPUT exception_valid_until
| where isnull(exception_valid_until) OR strptime(exception_valid_until, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S") < now()
| eval evidence_id = sha256(rule_name . event_id . _time)
| eval owner = coalesce(owner, "UNASSIGNED")
| table _time, rule_name, urgency, nis2_tier, status, owner, hours_since_detection, clock_state, src, dest, risk_object, evidence_id
| sort - hours_since_detection
cimSpl:
| tstats summariesonly=true count from datamodel=Alerts.Alerts where Alerts.severity IN ("high","critical") AND Alerts.status!="closed" AND _time >= relative_time(now(), "-3d") by Alerts.severity, Alerts.signature, Alerts.owner, Alerts._time
| rename Alerts.* as *
| eval hours_since_detection = round((now() - _time) / 3600, 2)
| where hours_since_detection >= 12
| sort - hours_since_detection
description: Detects ES notables raised at high or critical urgency that map to a NIS2-significant incident threshold and are approaching or have breached the Art.23(4)(a) 24-hour early-warning clock measured from notable creation, after excluding any time-bounded approved exceptions.
value: Missing the 24-hour early-warning window forfeits the supervisory authority's good-faith presumption under Art.32(4)(a) and triggers the Annex II inquiry obligations. This UC gives the Head of IR an auditable clock per significant incident so the early-warning is drafted before the deadline, not reconstructed afterwards.
implementation: (1) Onboard the `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv` lookup mapping each correlation `rule_name` to its NIS2 tier (Annex I/II/Implementing Reg 2024/2690 thresholds). (2) Schedule the SPL every 15 minutes over the last 3 days. (3) Configure `evidence_id` summary indexing to `audit_evidence`. (4) Send PagerDuty page on `clock_state=WARN_T_MINUS_4H`, escalate on `BREACH`. (5) Throttle by `rule_name` for 4 hours so a single noisy rule does not re-page during one campaign.
visualization: Row 1: Single-value tile `Significant incidents in clock window` (red ≥1). Row 2: Timeline panel showing notable aging with three threshold bands (12h, 20h, 24h). Row 3: Sortable table (rule_name, urgency, owner, hours_since_detection, status, drilldown to ES Incident Review for the notable). Row 4: 14-day trellis of `clock_state` counts per `nis2_tier` for capacity-review meetings.
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration on the `Alerts` data model with a 7-day retention. The cimSpl variant is 5–20× faster than the raw macro at fleet steady state because the `notable` macro joins against the `incident_review` KV collection per row; the DMA pre-aggregates this. Use the raw macro variant only when investigating an outlier and you need the un-aggregated `comment` / `status_description` text.

references.title: Splunk ES Notable Event Framework documentation
Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2024/2690 — significant incident thresholds
ENISA NIS2 Technical Implementation Guidance — incident notification

knownFalsePositives: 1) **Active major incident with declared crisis state** — analysts deliberately leave many notables open while triaging the underlying campaign. Distinguish: an open ServiceNow MAJ ticket and `risk_score > 250` on the parent risk object in `index=risk` for the same time window. Suppress: open a single time-bound exception in `nis2_exception_register.csv` keyed on the parent campaign rule_name for the duration of the declared crisis; do not suppress the rule globally. 2) **ES Risk-Based Alerting consolidating individual rule fires into an aggregate notable** — per-rule notables disappear and only the aggregate `Risk Analysis` notable fires. Distinguish: `urgency=informational` on the per-rule notable but `index=risk` shows recent contributions for that rule_name. Suppress: pivot the SPL to count risk-aggregated notables instead via the `Risk` data model. 3) **Bulk re-tuning after correlation-search release** — analysts re-triage while the rule's `lastModified` is recent. Distinguish: `| rest /servicesNS/-/SA-ThreatIntelligence/saved/searches | search title=<rule_name> | table updated`. Suppress: 7-day time-bound exception in `nis2_exception_register.csv`. 4) **Holiday weekend / unfilled on-call shift** — no analyst has acknowledged. Distinguish: cross-check `index=pagerduty sourcetype=pagerduty:incident` for an unacknowledged page tied to the notable. Do NOT suppress — the aged notable is a legitimate IR-drill finding and should escalate the on-call schedule. 5) **Notable raised via the `Risk Analysis` correlation search at urgency=informational** — collected for context, not paged. Distinguish: `urgency="informational"`. Suppress: filter `urgency IN ("high","critical")` in the SPL (already enforced). 6) **Forwarder clock skew on the indexer that received the notable** — `_time` is hours behind real time. Distinguish: `| eval lag=_indextime-_time | where lag > 1800` returns rows. Investigate via `chronyc sources` on the source. Suppress: use `_indextime` instead of `_time` for the clock calculation only when forwarder skew is documented in `nis2_known_skew_hosts.csv`.
exclusions: Does not classify whether the incident actually meets the Art.23(1) significance threshold (the lookup encodes the operator's pre-classified mapping; classification is a legal determination by the IR lead). Does not draft the early-warning notice text, does not submit the notice to the competent authority or CSIRT, does not establish the operator's NIS2 entity scope, and does not replace counsel review of cross-border, sectoral, or special-status notification channels.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.1` row-per-finding output, `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:saved_search` archive (7-year retention, RFC 3161 TSA-signed via `evidenceSigning`), and the `NIS2 Art.23 — 24h clock` panel on dashboard `compliance/nis2-art23-posture.xml`. Daily PDF digest delivered to `head-of-ir@<entity>` and `csirt-liaison@<entity>` archived to the same evidence index.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Fixture `sample-data/uc-22.2.1-fixture.json` injects a notable with `rule_name="NIS2-Significant-Ransomware"`, `urgency="critical"`, `status="In Progress"`, `_time=now()-21h`, lookup match `nis2_significant="yes"`, no exception. The search returns one row with `clock_state="WARN_T_MINUS_4H"`, `hours_since_detection`≈21, and a populated `evidence_id`.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Same fixture but with `status="Closed"` and a closure timestamp at `now()-19h`, OR with a non-expired entry in `nis2_exception_register.csv` for the rule_name. The search returns 0 rows. Verifies that closed notables and time-bound approved exceptions correctly suppress.

detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) ≥7.0 installed on a dedicated Search Head or Search Head Cluster. Confirm with `| rest /services/apps/local | search title=SplunkEnterpriseSecuritySuite | table version`.
• Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621) ≥5.3 installed on the SH and on indexers (for parsing).
• Splunk Add-on for Phantom (Splunkbase 3411) ≥3.0 if you want SOAR case enrichment (optional but recommended).
• Roles: the user running this search needs `read` on `index=notable` AND `read` on the `incident_review` KV-store collection (ES 7.0+). Add to a custom role `nis2_evidence_reader`; do not grant `ess_admin`.
• A populated lookup at `etc/apps/SA-NIS2/lookups/nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv` mapping each ES correlation `rule_name` to (`nis2_tier`: 'essential'|'important', `nis2_significant`: 'yes'|'no'|'context-dependent') per the operator's pre-classified Annex I/II self-assessment. The mapping is the operator's legal interpretation and must be approved by counsel before this UC's findings are used as evidence.
• A populated lookup at `etc/apps/SA-NIS2/lookups/nis2_exception_register.csv` with columns (`rule_name`, `exception_valid_until` ISO 8601, `approver`, `justification`). Exceptions are time-bound and require IR-lead and SME sign-off.
• Network: the SH must reach the source notable index (typically same SH for ES; cross-cluster only if Federated Search is enabled).
• License: each notable produces ~3 KB of result-summary into `audit_evidence` per scheduled run. At a 15-minute cadence and steady-state ~50 high/critical notables/day, expect ~3 MB/day to `audit_evidence`. Plan headroom and plan the 7-year retention.
• Baseline knowledge: the operator's NIS2 entity tier (essential vs important — affects whether the 24h clock applies via Art.23(1) AT ALL — and the operator's pre-classified mapping of which correlation rules represent significant incidents per Implementing Regulation (EU) 2024/2690.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
• ES Notable framework: confirm `Settings > Data Inputs > Notable Index Setup` shows `index=notable` (or the KV-store collection on ES 7.0+). Confirm correlation searches are enabled and producing notables: `| rest /servicesNS/-/SA-ThreatIntelligence/saved/searches | search action.notable.severity=high OR action.notable.severity=critical | table title disabled cron_schedule next_scheduled_time`.
• Lookup deployment: ship `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv` and `nis2_exception_register.csv` via a `SA-NIS2` app to the SHC deployer (`etc/shcluster/apps/SA-NIS2/lookups/`); the deployer pushes to all SH cluster members. Alternative: place the files in `etc/system/lookups/` for single-SH deployments. Verify with `| inputlookup nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv | head 5`.
• Optional SOAR enrichment: Splunk Add-on for Phantom must have a configured Phantom server and a read-only API token. Confirm `| inputlookup nis2_incident_link.csv | head 5` returns rows linking ES `event_id` to SOAR `container_id`.
• Expected event volume: 1 row per high/critical notable currently in the clock window. Steady state: 5–50 rows/run depending on correlation-rule tuning.
• Key fields validated by the search: `rule_name`, `urgency`, `status`, `owner`, `_time`, `event_id`. The `notable` macro provides them; if the macro returns columns but `status` is null, see Troubleshooting Step 5.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Raw-event SPL (the `spl` field of this UC). Save as a scheduled search named `UC-22.2.1 NIS2 Art.23 24h Clock`.

```spl
`notable` urgency IN ("high","critical") earliest=-3d
| eval hours_since_detection = round((now() - _time) / 3600, 2)
| lookup nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv rule_name OUTPUT nis2_tier nis2_significant
| eval nis2_significant = coalesce(nis2_significant, "unclassified")
| where nis2_significant != "no" AND status != "Closed"
| eval clock_state = case(
    hours_since_detection >= 24, "BREACH",
    hours_since_detection >= 20, "WARN_T_MINUS_4H",
    hours_since_detection >= 12, "NOTICE_T_MINUS_12H",
    1==1, "OK")
| where clock_state != "OK"
| lookup nis2_exception_register.csv rule_name OUTPUT exception_valid_until
| where isnull(exception_valid_until) OR strptime(exception_valid_until, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S") < now()
| eval evidence_id = sha256(rule_name . event_id . _time)
| eval owner = coalesce(owner, "UNASSIGNED")
| table _time, rule_name, urgency, nis2_tier, status, owner, hours_since_detection, clock_state, src, dest, risk_object, evidence_id
| sort - hours_since_detection
```

Understanding this SPL — design choices that survive review:
• **3-day window** so incidents that linger past 24h are still surfaced for the after-action evidence pack; the per-row `clock_state` distinguishes WARN from BREACH so the schedule needn't drop them.
• **Significance lookup before the time filter** so any notable not classifiable as significant is dropped early — auditors care about NIS2-significant only.
• **`nis2_significant != "no"` vs `== "yes"`** keeps `unclassified` and `context-dependent` rules visible; counsel decides per-incident whether the clock applies. Surfacing them is the operator-side evidence; suppressing them would hide legal-judgement calls.
• **`status != "Closed"`** because a closed notable means the IR team disposed of the incident; the clock only matters while the response is open.
• **Three threshold bands (12h, 20h, 24h)** so the team gets early-warning at T-12h (notice), T-4h (page), and T+0 (breach) — each tier has its own runbook step. The bands derive from the operator's internal SLA, not the directive itself; the directive only specifies T+24h.
• **`exception_valid_until` filter** so time-bound approved exceptions disappear from the result set automatically when they expire — mistakes that expose forgotten exceptions are caught.
• **`evidence_id = sha256(...)`** produces a stable, immutable identifier per finding for the audit trail. Auditors can pivot on `evidence_id` in `index=audit_evidence` to confirm the finding was archived.
• **`coalesce(owner, "UNASSIGNED")`** so unassigned notables surface with an explicit value — `null` owner is itself a finding (the IR team did not triage).

CIM-accelerated variant (the `cimSpl` field) — use this on fleet-scale deployments where the notable macro takes >30 seconds. The `Alerts` data model pre-aggregates and is 5–20× faster, but loses access to `comment`, `status_description`, and `risk_object` (those are not in the CIM model).

Schedule and alert action:
• Cron: `*/15 * * * *` (every 15 minutes); time range `-3d to now`.
• Alert action: `Number of results > 0`. Throttle by `rule_name` for 4 hours.
• Notification: PagerDuty Events API v2 with severity `warning` for `clock_state=WARN_T_MINUS_4H`, severity `critical` for `BREACH`. Annotate with the `evidence_id` and a deep link to the ES Incident Review page for the notable.
• Summary indexing: write each result row to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:saved_search` (Edit Alert Actions > Summary Indexing). Sign with RFC 3161 TSA in the saved search's alert action via the `splunk-add-on-rfc3161` app or post-process by a SOAR playbook.

Step 3 — Validate (parity checks before enabling the alert)
• Compare the notable count to the ES Incident Review queue for the same time window: `\`notable\` urgency IN ("high","critical") earliest=-3d | stats count` should equal `Splunk ES > Incident Review > Filter (Severity=High,Critical, Time=Last 3 days)`. Mismatch >5% means the macro and the ES UI disagree — investigate macro definition or KV-store sync.
• Spot-check two recent notables: pick two by `rule_name` from the last 24h. Open ES Incident Review, locate the same notable; confirm `urgency`, `status`, `owner` match what the SPL outputs. Mismatch on `status` typically means the user lacks `read` on the `incident_review` KV-store.
• Confirm CIM tagging if you intend to use the `cimSpl` variant: `\`notable\` | head 1 | table eventtype tag::eventtype` should include `notable` and tag `alert`. Missing tags means the CIM Add-on is not on the SH or its `tags.conf` is overridden.
• Run a 24h `timechart count` to find silent gaps: `\`notable\` urgency IN ("high","critical") earliest=-24h@h | timechart span=1h count` should not have flat-zero hours unless the SOC really had no high-urgency events. Flat zeros usually mean correlation searches stopped firing (skipped jobs) or a license breach.
• Lookup completeness: `| inputlookup nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv | stats count` should match the count of high/critical correlation searches in your ES instance: `| rest /servicesNS/-/SA-ThreatIntelligence/saved/searches | search action.notable.severity=high OR action.notable.severity=critical | stats count`. Diff means the operator has correlation searches that have NOT been classified for NIS2 significance — surface to IR lead for classification.
• Exception-register hygiene: `| inputlookup nis2_exception_register.csv | where strptime(exception_valid_until, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S") > now() | stats count by approver` should be < 10 active exceptions; more than that suggests scope creep.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard `compliance/nis2-art23-posture.xml` (recommended layout):
• **Row 1 — Headline tiles:** Single-value `Significant in 24h-clock window` (red ≥1), Single-value `Average hours_since_detection`, Single-value `Unassigned significant notables`.
• **Row 2 — Aging timeline:** Timeline panel with three threshold lines (12h notice, 20h warn, 24h breach). X-axis: `_time`. Y-axis: `hours_since_detection`. Colour by `clock_state`.
• **Row 3 — Action queue:** Sortable table with deep-link drilldown to ES Incident Review per notable. Columns: rule_name, urgency, nis2_tier, owner, hours_since_detection, status, evidence_id.
• **Row 4 — 14-day capacity view:** Trellis of `clock_state` counts per `nis2_tier` for the operations review meeting.
• **Time picker:** Default `Last 4 hours` for IR shift handover; preset `Last 7 days` for the weekly Head-of-IR review.
• **Access:** restrict to roles `nis2_evidence_reader`, `ir_analyst`, `head_of_ir`, `csirt_liaison`. Notable details may include sensitive PII or service identifiers.

Runbook (owner: Head of IR):
1. PagerDuty page on `WARN_T_MINUS_4H`. The IR shift lead opens the notable in ES Incident Review.
2. Confirm the notable's `nis2_tier` against the lookup; if `unclassified` or `context-dependent`, escalate to IR lead for legal interpretation BEFORE the clock breaches.
3. If `nis2_significant=yes`: open or update the early-warning draft in the NIS2 reporting workspace (typically a structured form in ServiceNow GRC or an internal portal). The draft must include: incident category, suspected cause, cross-border indicator, approximate impact scope.
4. CSIRT liaison forwards the early-warning to the national CSIRT via the secure notification channel (typically MeliCERTes / email PGP / national portal — varies by member state).
5. Annotate the notable's `comment` field with the early-warning submission timestamp. The annotation triggers the next-stage UC (UC-22.2.7 for the 72h notification).
6. Capacity-review monthly: query `| where _time>=relative_time(now(),"-30d") | stats count by clock_state, nis2_tier` and review with the IR drill schedule. Recurring `BREACH` for one rule_name typically indicates either a noisy correlation rule (re-tune) or a runbook gap (re-train).

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **`\`notable\` | head 1` returns no events.** ES is not installed on this SH OR the user's role lacks `read` on `index=notable`. Confirm with `| rest /services/apps/local | search title=SplunkEnterpriseSecuritySuite | table title version`. Add `read` for `index=notable` and the `incident_review` KV collection to the user's role.
• **All `urgency` values come back as `informational`.** ES Risk-Based Alerting (RBA) is enabled and individual rule fires are suppressed; only the aggregate `Risk Analysis` notable is raised, and that defaults to `informational`. Either: (a) raise the urgency on the `Risk Analysis` correlation search to `high` via `Configure > Content Management > Risk Analysis`; or (b) pivot the SPL to `index=risk` and aggregate by `(risk_object, source)`.
• **`status` field is `null` on most notables in ES 7.0+.** The KV-store-backed `incident_review` collection is the source of truth for status post-7.0; the user lacks `read` on the collection. Confirm with `| inputlookup incident_review_lookup | head 1`. Add the role permission via `Settings > KV Store > Collections > incident_review > Permissions`.
• **Notable counts in Splunk match Incident Review but not SOAR ticket counts.** Not every notable opens a SOAR ticket — only those above the SOAR playbook trigger threshold. To validate the SOAR side, check `index=phantom sourcetype=phantom:container | stats count by label` and reconcile with the SOAR playbook configuration.
• **`hours_since_detection` is negative or absurdly large.** Forwarder clock skew on the indexer that originally indexed the notable, OR `_time` was overridden by a downstream `eval _time = strptime(...)` in a custom correlation search. Diagnose with `\`notable\` | eval lag=_indextime-_time | stats avg(lag), p95(lag), max(lag)`. Hosts with `lag > 1800` need NTP/chrony repair via `chronyc sources`. As a stopgap, switch the clock calculation to `_indextime`.
• **After upgrading ES from 6.x to 7.x, `rule_name` is empty on new notables.** ES 7.3+ moved the rule_name lookup to a JSON config in the KV store; old saved-search definitions still reference the legacy CSV. Re-import the macro from the current `SA-Threatintelligence` app (`splunk reload deploy-server` if cluster-managed). Verify with `| rest /services/data/macros/notable | table definition`.
• **`nis2_tier` is `null` for many notables.** The `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv` lookup does not have an entry for that `rule_name`. Either the rule was added recently and not classified yet, or the lookup was not promoted from staging. Surface to IR lead for classification; the row will appear in the search output as `nis2_significant="unclassified"` so it is visible.
• **PagerDuty pages fire after the breach but not at WARN_T_MINUS_4H.** The schedule cron is `0 */1 * * *` (hourly) instead of `*/15 * * * *`; a 4h-wide warning window is missed by hourly schedules ~25% of the time. Set cron to `*/15 * * * *`.
grandmaExplanation: We watch every serious cyber alert and start a 24-hour clock the moment it is raised, so the team can warn the regulator before the clock runs out — not after, when it is too late.
equipmentModels: Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, notable events and incident response workflow)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, incident and change request data)
Splunk SOAR 6.x (Splunkbase 3411, automated playbook for notification workflow)
